reasoning: The NBER determines recession start dates based on significant, broad-based declines in economic activity, typically highlighted by consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth and falling employment. Throughout 2023 and 2024, US GDP growth remained consistently positive across every single quarter (e.g., Q1 2024 +1.4%, Q2 +3.0%, Q3 +2.8%, Q4 +2.4%), and employment held stable. While Q1 of 2025 experienced a minor -0.5% contraction primarily due to an import surge tied to tariff disruptions, Q2 2025 rebounded exceptionally well with 3.0% positive growth. With such consistently strong growth and job data spanning 2023-2024, it is fundamentally implausible that the NBER would retrospectively identify any part of 2023 or 2024 as the beginning of a recession. The prediction market aligns perfectly with this view, pricing the probability of an affirmative NBER declaration at just 2% as of August 2025. Given that we are already over halfway through 2025 with complete, robust data for 2023 and 2024, I cap my probability at the lowest allowable estimate of 2%.
